column5: Samak removed (cooking show payments). PPP dissolved Dec 2008. 109 PPP executives banned 5 years.
column6: Three major anti-elected rulings in one year. Led directly to Abhisit becoming PM via parliamentary maneuver. Court acting as kingmaker against Thaksin-aligned parties.

column5: No major rulings against or for government.
column6: Abhisit (Democrat) in power with establishment support. No need for court intervention against government.

column5: No major Constitutional Court rulings.
column6: Red Shirt crackdown managed by military, not courts. Court quiet during political crisis.

column5: No major rulings. Yingluck wins July landslide — no immediate court action.
column6: Court accepts civilian election result without challenge. Unusual period of judicial restraint.

column5: No major rulings against government.
column6: Yingluck governs. Court quiet.

column5: Court rules against Yingluck government's constitutional amendment attempt (Senate restructure) Nov 2013.
column6: Single but significant ruling. Blocked Pheu Thai from changing Senate composition. Coincides with PDRC protest escalation.

column5: May 7: Yingluck removed for abuse of power (Thawil transfer case). Coup followed May 22.
column6: Court removal of PM preceded and enabled coup. Pattern: court destabilizes elected government, military completes the removal.

column5: No rulings against government. Junta in power — no oppositional targets.
column6: Court not needed as political tool when military runs government directly.

column5: Court approves new junta-drafted constitution via referendum oversight.
column6: Court legitimizes junta constitutional project. No anti-elected rulings — no elected government exists.

column5: New constitution takes effect. Court operates within junta framework.
column6: Continued junta period. Court dormant as anti-elected instrument.

column5: No major rulings. Courts dismiss several existing LM cases (TLHR data).
column6: Unusual lull in court political activity. TLHR documented courts dismissing cases rather than prosecuting.

column5: Feb: Thai Raksa Chart dissolved for nominating royal candidate for PM. Sep: Prayuth ruled eligible to continue as PM despite military background.
column6: Two significant rulings — one removes electoral threat (Thai Raksa Chart), one protects junta PM continuation. Both favor establishment.

column5: Feb 21: Future Forward Party dissolved (Thanathorn loan = illegal donation). Dec: Prayuth acquitted of ethics breach over army housing.
column6: FFP dissolution triggers mass pro-democracy protests. Prayuth acquittal protects establishment PM. Both rulings favor military-royalist bloc.

column5: Nov 10: Court rules monarchy reform calls constitute attempt to overthrow democratic system with King as Head of State. Constitutional amendment (135,000 voter signatures) rejected.
column6: Landmark ruling criminalizes pro-democracy movement's core demand. Prachatai: 'calls for reform count as overthrowing the regime.'

column5: Aug: Prayuth suspended over 8-year term limit. Sep: Returns after favorable ruling — court finds term counts from 2017 constitution, not 2014.
column6: Suspension creates instability but ruling ultimately protects Prayuth. Net effect: pro-establishment despite temporary suspension.

column5: Jul: Pita Limjaroenrat suspended from parliament (media shares). Court accepts MFP dissolution petition. MFP blocked from forming government despite election win.
column6: Systematic lawfare against election winner. Pattern mirrors 2008 (Thaksin allies) and 2020 (FFP). Khaosod: 'Lawfare has begun; Pita's fate repeats Thailand's political turmoil.'

column5: Aug 7: Move Forward Party dissolved — leaders banned 10 years. Aug 14: PM Srettha removed (appointed ex-convict Pichit to cabinet). Nov 22: Sedition case against Thaksin/Pheu Thai dismissed.
column6: Two major anti-elected rulings in one week (Aug 7 and Aug 14). Most concentrated court activity since 2008. Nov dismissal is rare pro-Pheu Thai outcome — mixed signal.

column5: Aug 29: PM Paetongtarn removed — ethical breach over Hun Sen phone call conduct.
column6: Second consecutive PM removed by court within 13 months. Court directly intersects with T5 Cambodia provocation signal — Hun Sen phone call is both T6 channel health event and T2 trigger.

column5: No major rulings recorded to date. Anutin government installed without court intervention.
column6: Post-Paetongtarn period. Court activity reduced — new government not yet a court target.
